18 - I Gave My Top Sales Rep My Car - YouTube.mp4
What we do at Seventh Level for salespeople is the top three salespeople. Every two months, out of those two months, we don't record it— every two months gets to drive the car. So JP is our top closer, top salesperson. Paul is our top SDR— the last two months. So these guys work remotely. So they fly in, they get to drive my car anywhere they want to for three days, or they can drive the Range Rover, the Benz, wherever you want. So it's a lot of fun to sell a seventh level. You might want to try it because you can really get good at sales if you sell a seventh level too. All right. I'm here with JP Egan. So you're now the number one closer at seventh level.
And we have like, I want to say 48, 49 salespeople. So well done. It wasn't always this way though. So I want everybody to know, what is your story? When did you actually get into sales? Yeah. Well, I appreciate you for having me on number one. A hundred percent. Yeah. When it comes to when I got into sales, first of all, it was January of 2024 when I actually got into sales. Not a long time ago. Yeah. Yeah. But before that, I was actually stacking shells in the supermarket. So basically working supermarket nine to five, I was doing that. Okay, what's the name of the supermarket you work for? It's called Super Valley. It's more of like an Irish supermarket. I used to live in Ireland. Yeah, you were in Ireland.
What part of Ireland? I lived in Ireland when I was 19. Dublin, yeah. Okay, right there in Dublin. I lived close to Trinity University. I was like a block away. There you go, yeah. My dad went to school. I always remember the Indian curry. I think the the smell from the Indian curry from from there in Trinity 100. So you're up in Dublin, you got this. You got the job. Was that like out of high school? Yeah, like that job. So for me, I got out of high school, and I literally just wanted to get a job. I wanted to get make money, some way or another, because I knew I wanted to make a lot of money. I didn't know exactly how always. You didn't know what to do. Exactly.
Yeah. Because my dad was actually, he owned a business with worth over 180 million at some point and everything went down. Okay. Right. It got to the point where it was me and my brother sleeping in the same bedroom as each other. Wow. Like I was 18, he was 22. So obviously not the best ages of our life to be living together. So, you know, what happened to you is. I'm assuming this would happen to you and this is really good. When your dad went down and you went from, like, a pretty lavish lifestyle, pretty comfortable as a kid, and then all of a sudden, everything shifted. Something in your brain probably said, 'Hey, I don't want to ever be like this or go through this.' Would I be right?
A hundred percent. And that, that was the thing when you see. The lifestyle he had— Ferrari, Porsche, all these different things— right? And you see, everything can go in a split second. It just shows you a lot. And for me, I knew I wanted to be successful. So I got the supermarket job. It was going to be a part-time gig, yeah. And later, than the year I got a nine-to-five, and when I got the nine-to-five, I was introduced to sales. It was actually funny enough—I was going on the train too much at five— and I see a video problem for you. Page. And it was your video, a 60-minute live keynote. Oh, like one of my, uh, Oh, we probably published a keynote on YouTube or something. Or something. Okay.
For me, because it was actually a little bit of a sales role, not like fully, but it was kind of, we sold SIM cards, you know, like $5 SIM cards. Yeah. Those things, nothing crazy, but like I was really passionate. About the job and come to me watching that video, I just transformed the way I looked at sales. Really, how did it transform the way you looked at sales? Because for me, it always was something that, like, as they say, you can 'the eyes to Eskimos' and all these different things that you do to people. And when you, when you look at, like, how passionate you were about it, what you were saying, especially one thing that really stuck with me was: 'Don't listen to what people are saying.
Listen to what they mean.' They say, and that's so powerful because there's such a deeper level behind it. And when I watched that keynote, it really gave me a whole understanding of what sales was, and that's when I really had the passion. So I'm going to get into sales, and that's going to be my fault. And you're not doing it to them. You're doing it for them, which is 99% of salespeople have never really had that concept in their brain. You know why it's so good? That it happened to you as a child with your dad because that gave you a chip on your shoulder. Yes, it did. Because had that not happened, you might not have this.
Drive necessarily as much as you do to really master selling and make as much, help as many people as you can and make as much money. You don't know if you have that drive. You probably still have a drive because your dad taught you that, but. Now you have a different level because now you have a chip on your shoulder. It's like Tom Brady. Everybody has a chip on their shoulder. You just got to activate it or... and you drive. Okay, so... you got, you watched this YouTube video of mine. When was that? That was back in, it was kind of December time. December of 2022. Yeah. Wow. That's crazy. Well, I think something important you said there was that you actually were reading out a stat of like the successful people in the world, billionaires and millionaires, what they actually did and it was all sales.
So when I heard that stat, I was like, 'This is a job I need to get into. I need to learn this. Yeah. I need to learn this. Okay. So walk us through. So you got a sales job before you came here. You got a couple of sales jobs. When did you first start? It's funny enough. I worked for like a kid. He was like 18 years old doing his own business. Nothing too crazy. And then we ended up, I think, at that time. He had a cold calling business. I would cold call the people, and then I would also go from cold calling them to booking them on my calendar. Okay, like 98 of people wouldn't show up, so I ended up making like 100 bucks that month.
So it was like a It was like a marketing agency or something. But at the time, I was also still working at the 9 to 5. So my schedule was like, because I was in UK time, so I was able to work in the nights for US. Yeah. I was waking up at 6 AM going. The office at 9 a. m. and then 5 p. m. to 12 p. m. So that was kind of my schedule. You had the dedication. So you did that for a while. Didn't really do that well because you didn't really have the training. Exactly right. I was selling them. It was kind of like an AI lead generation for them. Okay. Yeah. Get them more leads, more listings, more bookings. Yeah. And what type of training did you go through in these jobs? Nothing. It was, hey, just sell. For the best and you get my commission checks reflected that at the end yeah sell like a winger right then you didn't really know like hey i gotta i gotta master these so how did how did you get involved. You watched that video a while ago. You got a couple of sales jobs. You didn't like those. You left those. And how did you get in here with 7 Level? Yeah. Funny enough, I had a friend who was actually working for Iman Gazi at the time and he was in high ticket sales.
Okay. And he said, 'Apply for the 10 best companies you want to work at.' And obviously after seeing your video, I was really inspired. And regardless of just like the money you can make— from the company, the most important thing was the skill, learning the skill, knowing that if you have the skill, it's just impossible for you to go broke because you have the skill and you can use that anywhere you want. Exactly. So I applied and, luckily enough, at like 3 a. m. one day, I got an email. Anthony was already interviewed. So I was like, 'No way!' Okay. Our senior vice president, now what was he doing three years ago? I think he was maybe a salesman or something over recruiting and sales. So you got hired.
Did you start on the SDR team? Yes, I started as an SDR, but he told me, 'He was like, look, we'll start using SDR.' There's a potential that you could be a closer. I heard that word and that was all I needed to... I just got to perform. Exactly. Okay, so... When you got in here and we started training you, because you would have got more training from probably our sales managers and trainers more than me. You were going through the course, so you saw that from me. What changed for you once you really started learning? Yeah, it's the whole structure behind it. And I think, number one, understanding. not the script, but the principles of what you're trying to get out. Yeah.
Because I think a lot of people get stuck on like, okay, this is what I have to say. I have to say or ask this question. Exactly. But it's really more about what outcomes you're trying to get out of each stage of the APQ. When you understand at a high level, you know and understand how to have a conversation with a client and get them in a more emotional, deeper level because you actually build a connection with them. Yeah, because what that allows you to do, when you understand the psychology behind each stage, like why am I asking connection questions and what I'm looking for, then it allows you flexible. Exactly. It allows you to be flexible with the script. So when they give you an answer, because every prospect is going to say different things.
2% of the prospects are going to stay on script. That's just sales 101. You have to know how to tweak the next questions based off what they say in like a snap of a finger. And if you don't understand the psychology, you're just going to the next thing on your script and it doesn't connect emotionally. Exactly right. 100%. Okay, so going back here, other sales methodologies that you'd looked at before an APQ, what would you say the differences were? Like the main three differences. Yeah, the biggest difference. Was like, okay, it's product pushing. It's all about talking about the product, how amazing it is, and just like trying to push something to the prospect. It doesn't feel like you're working with the prospect to find a solution for them.
And that was the biggest difference I saw when I, that's why I resonated with NEPQ so much. Yeah. So that's one. What would you say are the two other big ones? So obviously, we're not selling the thing. We're selling the results of what the thing does. And that doesn't matter if you're selling cabinets to a homeowner. You're selling the results of how the cabinet makes, maybe, the wife feel when her neighbors come over and she sees these new cabinets or whatever it is. So you're selling what we call emotion. States, right? So give us a couple other examples. Yeah, like tonality. That was huge for me. I used to just be this straightforward guy, toned. And I see it in my conversations. I used to cause a lot of resistance with the prospect.
And I could see them with a guard the whole time, especially real estate agents. They were in sales, right? So with them, it was a tough conversation. And when I learned the tone and how to use it to get them in a more emotional... Deeper level and get the question to allow them to impact it and feel a lot more. It just transforms. Yeah, 100% because people, as you know, they don't buy off logic, they buy on emotions. Our studies prove that. There's no debate. So if you can't master emotionally connecting with them, and how do you do that? Well, it's one thing to say, 'I need to master emotional connection,' but how do you do that? Yes. So it's not necessarily just the questions you ask, but it's how you ask those questions.
It's your tone, because that's how they interpret. Yeah. Why you're asking the question in the first place, 100%. Okay, can you remember a specific sale where any PQ like really clicked and you're like, okay, I think I'm starting to get this. Yeah, it was actually at the time, it was my first month. That month was like one of my big, for me, it was crazy life-changing. I went from making $30,000 a year to $30,000 commissions that month. As a closer. Yes. Your first month as a closer. Because you were in SDR for like a month or two or something. No, it was like two weeks. Two weeks. Yes. And that started to stop. Quickly. Yes. Yes. Exactly. Okay. So yeah, I did $30,000 commission that month.
And the sale I remember was with this prospect where they were thanking me after the call. Where I felt like I used to always have to fight with them to get them. And then it would be like resistance at the end, like just trying to get the payment really quickly. And at the end, they were like, 'Thank you for selling them.' And it just blew my mind. I couldn't help but wonder how did you feel when that happened. I felt amazing because you realize that you're not doing it to people but you're doing it for people. Yeah, because, in our example, people come to us because they want to really learn how to sell more.
Yeah, companies come to us, people like, 'They know if they sell more, they get paid more.' I think that's the best part about this job is that it's not just about selling. It's that you're selling knowing that they're going to get an outcome that's amazing afterwards. Knowing that I've got the outcome myself, I can have so much conviction, certainty in what I actually sell. That's another thing that you can transfer to the prospects when you have that conviction, certainty in what you actually sell. A lot of people sell these products they don't even believe in. And that causes obviously a lot of resistance at the end. Yeah, it's hard to like help them overcome objections if you don't necessarily believe in what you're selling in the first place, right?
So you need to, I think you said it right, you need to find, you know, whatever you sell, like a... a service, a product, or a training like we do that actually gets people the results that you're saying you can get and you feel pretty confident that you know, in that, but if it doesn't get results. Morally, eventually it kind of wears out. Yeah, because you're speaking to humans. They can tell, right? Especially with the real estate thing. I had that kind of morally inside me. I knew they weren't 100% going to get the results or even have a higher likelihood, like we have over here, from that marketing exactly. Yeah. You know, one of the industries I sold in very shortly.
When one company I sold for couldn't get them the results, and like, hey, after about five months, I'm like, 'I'm out.' Because I can't do that and have a clear conscious. I've got to go to sleep at night. I've got to wake up in the morning and I'm only going to do it because I'm doing it for them. And if they're not getting the results, they're being promised by this company. That's a no-no, 100%. And it's crazy now because that first month as a closure, you made 30 grand. Now you're ever psyched. 70 or something like that. What's, what's the difference in your life now going from $30,000 a year to $7,000 a month in commissions? I completely changed my life.
Cause for me, like before I joined 7th, I was making $30,000 a year and now I'm making $7,000 a month. Yeah and yeah, like the changes in terms of lifestyle obviously that comes with it but the most important thing for me was being able to change my family's life, being able to give money to my father, make him feel like he's back where he was, being able to change my brother's life. My brother has become my appointment setter for his company and he averages around 15,000 to $20,000 a month as an appointment setter and being able to change. His life, introduce him to sales, and everything in the above is just life-changing for me. Yeah. What would you say?
I kind of asked you this a little bit earlier, but let's go back. What would you say were the top three things? Because you're still learning here. Here's the thing. We're committed to mastery, right? Mastery is an... learning process. You know I was meeting with Dean. I love Dean. I'm in his mastermind. Graziano— his last name is so hard for me to pronounce— but Tony Robbins, his business partner, I'm in a mastermind with him and Tony. I was having breakfast with him last week. And, you know, we were talking about, hey, like. We have the idea, like Tony was like, 'We know nothing.' We're a student for life. We know nothing. I'm still mastering this thing we call sales and, supposedly, had a pretty decent career. So I'm always mastering.
You're still learning. 100%. What are, recently, just in the last couple months, what are maybe... Two or three things that you've learned, because now I've kind of taken over the training, training you guys like three times a week. What are some things you were getting from me, just training you guys as a group, that you didn't know before? Well, something really, really important I learned last week is that like listening to the prospects. tone when you're about to close them because, what I used to do, is just do my reframe and just go for the close. But I didn't realize that you can actually tell with their tone when they're ready to close. And the issue is that if you ask the closing question without them actually being ready, and you continue to ask it, it's just going to build resistance in the prospect because they just feel you're just trying to get their money.
But if you ask at the right time when you hear their tone, it just comes off way more effectively. Yeah, 100%. Because I can listen to what they mean, not just what they're saying. Because if I said, 'Hey, do you feel like, if I ask you to give me emotion, do you feel like this could be the... answer for you?' And they're like, 'Yeah, uh, yeah.' 'Yeah.' Mostly like, 'Okay, great.' Let me show what to do. Well, what I just heard is uncertainty. Yes. So I'm going to be like. 'Hold on,' you seemed a bit... hesitant when I asked you that question. What's going on? You can tell me. 100%. And because I shift my tone to that concern tone and actually address it, they open up.
Like, 'Well, I'm not sure about this.' And so then I'm going to start to reframe them out of that concern and loop back around. And if they're not ready for that close, I'm going to have to reframe them again. And I'm going to have to keep looping back around until I hear their tone shift. And then I know I can go around. Yes. Well, the other thing was that you taught me. Identity selling at a high level is such a huge shift in in sales when you can actually know how to like place the prospect and the identity of the person who you need them to become, and they need that to become, to become a buyer. Cause that's huge. Like I'll just give an example.
So people already understand what we're talking about. And let's say someone who had a bad experience in the past. You taught me this the other week, where you would say, 'Hey, I really appreciate you for being in the call and still looking at other options, looking to invest yourself.' Because it shows a lot about who you are. Even though you've had a bad experience, you're not giving up. Because we all know those people who let that bad experience overcome them. What typically happens to those people? and just labeling them as the person who doesn't let that experience overcome them, so that doesn't stop them in the past. Yeah, 100%. It's the same thing. Hey, good for you for being on this call and being the...
Person that is, you know, responsible enough and for thinking to never give up because it sounds like you're the type of you're the person that never gives up. So that's the label. It sounds like you're the person that never gives up. So that's the label, first, right? You know, and your family's lucky to have somebody like you that never gives up. That is that forward-thinking. That knows that they're the ones that have to make the hard decisions for the family to get where they want to go because I talk to a lot of people. And you'd be surprised how many of them let like a negative experience of like a bad trainer, like cause them to really never master selling. You know what I mean? And we all know what happens.
Happens to those people that give up from a negative experience, they never go where they never get where they want. So for you, like being a person that never gives up, that is. Committed to your family to just master this. Is that something you were born with or something you learned? Yeah, I absolutely love that. You're building into that identity. Exactly. It's just because, again, you're getting them to be the person who they need to become to actually change. Yeah. Yeah. Super important. Sigmund Freud said it best. People do who they believe they are. Yep. So basically, they make their decisions based on the identity of the human being they believe they are. That's why I always say Alcoholics Anonymous doesn't work that well.
There's a reason why they have so many people that get into that program that relapse. Because they constantly say, 'I've been an alcoholic for 30 years,' or 'I've been sober for 30 years.' 30 years, or 'I'm an alcoholic for 10 years.' Well, they're still buying into that— that's their identity. I'm an alcoholic. So what identity are they going to make decisions based off? What an alcoholic does. But if I just started reframing myself and be like, you know what, I just decided I don't want to drink because it's not really good for me. And I just started talking like that, I start to move away from that identity of I'm an alcoholic. 100%. It's the same concept, right? Man, this is good stuff. Okay, what's your favorite objection to handle?
Um, I love the partner, right? We all know it's great to prevent it. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Um, I obviously love preventing the partner, but actually I do enjoy handling it too. Okay. So tell us why you like the partner. I remember I was really young in sales in the beginning. I would always love to get an objection because I'm like pouncing on. And then, as I got older, I'm like, 'Oh, I'm getting lazy.' I need to prevent this more. Then I started learning how to prevent them. So it's kind of fun. So why do you love that objection? Um, essentially because we understand that it's not a necessity. Cause I think what you taught us in the training is that if something happened where it was a necessity, would they go and speak to their partner about it?
No. Like if the AC goes out and it's 103 degrees in the summer, do you have to go ask your spouse? Would you go ask your spouse to fix AC? Yeah. Why? Because it's a... Necessity. Yeah. So let's go with that. I'm going to be the prospect. Sure, yeah. You be you. And we call that a necessity frame. Yeah. To reframe. All we're doing is we're taking... them out of this belief system that I need to talk with my spouse because half the time, do we really think they even talk to their spouse? No, probably not. No, it's just an excuse, right? Exactly, yeah. And the other half of the time, that spouse doesn't know necessarily all the problems they're having. They don't know all the issues.
So they're going to go there like, hey, you know, I talked with this. salesperson and it's this thing and it's going to cost this. So instead of in the spouse has no concept, but like, oh, it's just more money rather than like in our case. How much it's costing them in lost sales every week because they don't have the trail. It's like 100 times more money than that, you know? So let's go through that and kind of show it to them. Well, JP, I really... I really like this. I need to talk with my spouse and maybe we can see what she thinks. Get back together, you know, in a couple of days or so. Yeah. Well, I appreciate you for sharing that. I guess partner, let's say your partner was out of the equation.
Like, would you actually do it? You feel like we can help you with getting to where you need to be? Yeah, really. It sounds good. I know I got to really learn how to sell. I know I'm leaving money on the table for sure. I kind of realized that from the call. What do you really feel like it can for you personally, though? Well, I need to get better at disarming them. Right. You know, I have a hard time at the beginning of the call. They seem like so stiff and like surface up with me. And I know I'm not opening them up. I'm just—I just—I know I'm like losing at least three or four deals a week. That, because you disarming your prospects, what will that mean for your life?
Well, more sales. Yeah. I mean, more sales. I mean, that's why we're talking, you know, I just got to get her permission. I always run things. Yeah. And I, and I appreciate you for being on. Cause I guess the one thing I would share a different perspective on, on that is that when it comes to your partner, it also, when you having her, it comes with a level of responsibility. Would you agree? Yeah, yeah, I'm responsible. Yeah, yeah, 100%. I guess whose responsibility do you really feel like it is to learn the skills to put you and your partner in a better position? That would be mine. Yeah. Why though? Why would that be your responsibility? Well, yeah, I'm dead.
Man of the house, you know it'd be my you know it's my my job to provide you know, 100 because it's like— if the AC went out and it was 105 degrees, would you go out there and ask her for permission to pay that bill to be able to get it on, so you guys could have air conditioning in the house, or would you just go out and do it? Well, no, because it's, you know, like. You know, it's like we have to have it or we die. You know, it's kind of a necessity. Yeah, it's a necessity. And do you feel like learning the skills is going to be a necessity so you can provide a better life for you and your partner? Yeah, I can see that.
You can see that or you actually feel like it is? What do you mean? Well, with your tone, you sounded like you weren't really sure if it was a necessity. I guess, what's behind that? Well, maybe, maybe if she says no, I could just. You know, Eventually figure it out. I don't know. Yeah. Well, that's how you always do things, right? Yeah, we talked about that earlier. Yeah. But it's to get different results, we have to do things like the same way or do we have to do things differently? Yeah, you make a good point. Yeah. Well, what I'm not asking you to do is make a decision based on where you are. I'm asking you to make a decision based on where you actually want to be.
Well, I want to make more sales. There you go, right? Because the Jeremy who's already been making the money he wants to make, been able to change his family's life. If it was him in this position right here and now, do you feel like he would take that responsibility on his shoulders and make that decision for his family? Or does he put that responsibility on his partner? Yeah, he'd make that decision for the family, yeah. So what do you feel like you need to do for your family? Yeah, I like that. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So you're just leading him into it rather than like, you know, pressuring. Exactly. 100%. Yeah. Yeah. And I'm going to show you some objection prevention. Yeah.
Lot of times, even before we get there and we talk, and I think you're learning the objection prevention on that. Like when, when the spouse gets that objection, you know, and it's going to be a little bit different depending on the industry. But for us, when, you know, they give that objection, you're just basically finding out, well, how, how does your spouse feel about you losing? You're saying $10,000 a month in commissions? Yeah. See how my tone shifts into that concern tone? Because now I'm triggering more of an emotion there. Because I'm getting them to like, that sounds bad. Because it obviously is bad, right? And a lot of times they might say, 'Well, oh, she doesn't really know.' Or he doesn't really know.
If you told him that today, you were losing $10, 000 a month because of the skill level. What do you think she would be thinking? Yeah. See that soft tone, that kills them. And I'm not doing it to them, I'm doing it for them. Exactly, yeah. Because now they start to realize internally, like, 'Oh shit.' Yeah. This is my responsibility. And even if it's the lady boss of the house, you know, she wants to contribute. She wants to be independent. It's the same thing. And then, you know. What does she... Want you to like master this so you can Bring that extra $10,000 a month into the family. Yeah. They're not going to say no. Exactly. Yeah, she would.
Because, I mean, what would you guys be able to do with that that you can't do now? Yeah. You know, that tone shift, okay? You know, well, we'd be able to do this, you know, blah, blah, blah. And then later in a little bit of that call, maybe problem awareness, solution awareness, I'm going to look for another opportunity to then put in that identity frame. Well, you know, good for you at looking at doing something about this now. I mean, your wife and your kids are, you know, they're lucky. To have a provider like you— that is, that forward-thinking and responsible, and knows they have to make the hard decisions themselves for the family—is something I talk to a lot of people about.
You'd be surprised how many of them, they just, you know, they say they want to sell more, but then they just kind of— They never do anything about learning the skills to do it. And now they put that family, the burden and the stress, on their family financially. You know what I mean? Oh, no, that's not me. I'm not like that. I mean, for you, like being that forward-thinking, that responsible, like knowing you have to make these hard decisions yourself for your family. Is that something you were born with or something you had to learn? Yeah. And then you're labeling them. Yeah, see, so I'm labeling them and I'm not doing it to them, I'm doing it for them. Exactly. But you see, I started that.
Like your spouse, they're lucky to have, if I'm talking to the provider, a provider like you that is that forward-thinking, that responsible, and knows they have to make the hard decisions themselves for their family. Yep, because you're rewarding. Those that have to make the hard decisions themselves. Exactly. You know, that's in that frame. So that's that positive identity. Nobody's ever going to argue that. Like, no, I'm not forward-thinking or responsible, you know? They're like, even if they're not, they're going to be like, 'Yeah, that's me.' And then I put in that negative identity. I mean, I talk to a lot of people, and you'd be surprised how many of them, they just... They say they want to sell more, but then they never do anything about it.
And they leave all that burden and stress financially on their spouse's shoulders. You know what I mean? That's even a better. And it's so powerful because. It's from a third view perspective. And then they're the ones telling themselves why they do not want to be like that. Yeah. And they're like, 'Oh, no, no, I would never do that to my spouse.' I mean, for you, then I'm going to strengthen the frame again. For you. Being that responsible, that forward-thinking, knowing you have to make these hard decisions for your family, is that something you were born with or something you had to learn? So no matter if I've always been that way or know I had to learn it, they're just strengthening that new identity. Yeah. So that's, it's perfect, man.
You just, that's, I love the necessity frame and there's some different angles that we'll go over some other time. Um, what advice would you give to your past self before joining seventh level as a salesperson? Yeah. I guess for me, the biggest thing that I felt like I wish I did was just learning the skills earlier. It took me like a while to get to the level where I was. And I think if I just committed to the skills and committed to mastery, I would have just gone their way quicker. I like to use an analogy, right? It's like you can walk to LA or you can fly, right? Which will get there quicker. Obviously you have to pay to fly. Yes, that is something you do have to do.
But the issue is, especially in sales and jobs where we're in sales, what do we leave on the table every single step we take? And every time we're not on that plane, and that's money. I think you said something really brilliant there. That's your COI, cost of inaction. Yes. So what is the cost of inaction every single week, every single month, and lost sales that you lose every single week, every single month, month after month, year after year? Yeah. Rather than just investing. One time into the training, End. Now in the next few months, you don't lose those sales. Exactly. That's like a hundred X return, you know, comparatively speaking. So you're smart. That's why you got here very, very quickly. People never get there because they just stay as a winger.
They stay as a dabbler and just kind of dabble in it where they think they know everything and they never commit to mastery. They never get to that level. And mastery is still something we're doing. Okay. A couple of last questions here. What advice would you give to just a new salesperson getting into any type of sales job? What advice would you give to them besides just... That because that's really good advice you just gave yeah like it's it's hard because fundamentally it all comes down to skills it's all about learning the skills if you do not have the skills you're not going to be able to put yourself in a position where you're where you're going to be making the money that you need to and putting yourself in the positions that you want to be in yeah i would always especially like if we're not talking about the skills we're talking about something else like one thing that i always stood by when i first came into seventh level was
At the very beginning, I know it will take time to learn the skill. So what I have to do is allow my work ethic to overcome my skill set at the very beginning. Yeah. Because you can't just have great skills and be that lazy. Two hours a week. Right, exactly. And I have to consistently put in the work. So at the very beginning, while I was learning the skills, I allowed my work ethic to overcome the lack of skill I had at the beginning to put myself in a position where I could earn the 30 grand, 70 grand, all those. Yeah, it's so true. I always say it's a skills game, not a numbers game. But in the beginning, where you're learning the skills, you have to go through more numbers.
Exactly. And you said it brilliantly. As you develop those skills, it becomes less and less and less of a numbers game. It's funny because I actually worked seven days a week when I first came in. I could now work five and make more because of this skill. Exactly. You can work. It's so true. Like a lot of salespeople, you know, they'll be like, 'Oh my gosh, I'm killing myself working 60 hours a week.' When I started to really understand selling, making a lot of money per year. I was working less than 40 hours a week, sometimes 30 hours a week, and I was making $200,000, $250,000 every month in commissions. It was because I understood not even just the skill level at that time, but I knew how to manage my calendar more effectively.
I knew income-producing activities. I didn't waste time. So a lot of salespeople go into the office and they'll be like, 'I work 12 hours a day.' And you're like, 'Actually, you talk to about four hours a day of prospects.' Yes. So you work four hours. Exactly. What were you doing the other eight? Social media, hanging out, a two-hour lunch. That's not work. Yeah. So it's the income-reducing activities, that schedule, like you have it. With the skill level, it's really really hard to fail selling— like almost impossible yes. 100%. Any last words of advice you'd like to give anybody else besides that? Yeah, I would just say, like, when it comes to number one, um, I just want to appreciate everything in terms of the company this company that we've built.
I think it's just life-changing for a lot of people. I think if you're not in what you're doing, right? And what I would say is that, like, especially if you're wanting to come work for Seventh Level. The one thing I fundamentally stood by is, even if I don't make the money I want to, what am I doing? I'm learning the skills. Now, regardless of what happens with my life, I know I can never go broke because I learned the skills. And that is going to be the most important part about this interview. A hundred percent. And congratulations, you now own shares of the company. And at the end of every year, we take our four top salespeople and we also issue shares to them as well.
And since you're the number one guy, as long as you continue that— the next five months— you're going to get even more shares at the end of every year. So it's crazy to be able to say this at 21, have a company, they provide that for you. So it's insane. And yeah, I'm grateful. That's important, man. All right. Love you.
